questions.explanation: Short U. One 'u' in a closed syllable (bus) → SHORT. Same vowel as in zus, kus, nummer.

options: Long rounded vowel — lips like 'oo', tongue like 'ee'
questions.explanation: Lange UU. Round your lips for 'oo', then say 'ee' without moving them. Same sound as German ü or French 'u'.

questions.q: Do 'au' and 'ou' sound different in Dutch?
options: No — they sound identical, both like English 'ow' in 'now'
Yes — only at the end of words
questions.explanation: AU and OU are pronounced IDENTICALLY in Dutch — both [ow], like English 'ow' in 'now'. The spelling is etymological. Blauw and mevrouw rhyme.

questions.q: Which word does NOT rhyme with 'mevrouw'?
questions.explanation: 'muur' is long UU [muur]. The other three are all OU/AU [ow] — they rhyme. Listen for the diphthong vs the pure long vowel.
OU = [ow]. Final 'd' devoices to 't'. 'koud' = [kowt]. Same with 'goud' [khowt] and 'oud' [owt].

questions.q: Why does 'u-ren' (in 'tien uren') sound the same as 'uur'?
options: Open syllable rule: 'u' alone in an open syllable is long, same as 'uu' in a closed one
questions.explanation: Both produce the same long UU sound. 'uur' uses double-u in a closed syllable; 'u-ren' uses one 'u' in an open syllable. Spelling adapts, sound stays the same.

questions.q: Which spelling rule decides if 'u' is short or long?
options: Whether the syllable is closed (one consonant after = short) or open / has 'uu' (= long)
There's no rule — you just memorise it
questions.explanation: Closed syllable + single u = SHORT (bus, kus, nummer). Open syllable + single u, OR double 'uu' = LONG (u-ren, muur, duur). Same rule as for a/e/o/i.
